title: Offshore Energy Security Act of 2025
plain_summary: This law requires the government to hold oil and gas lease sales in the Gulf of Mexico twice a year for ten years. Each sale must offer at least 74 million acres of ocean area for companies to drill for oil and gas. The law also bans new drilling in areas close to Florida's coast and other sensitive ocean areas until 2035. Companies that already have leases in those banned areas can keep them, and some areas can be used for environmental protection projects instead.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Offshore Energy Security Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Offshore Oil and Gas Lease Sales
sections.text: This section explains what the Secretary of the Interior must do to sell leases for oil and gas drilling in ocean waters. An offshore lease sale means the government sells rights to drill for oil and gas in ocean areas. The Secretary is the head of the Interior Department. The Secretary must follow the same rules and terms used in a Gulf of Mexico lease sale that happened in November two thousand twenty three. When companies make good bids on ocean areas, the Secretary must give out leases within ninety days to the highest bidders. The Secretary can skip other requirements that might slow down these lease sales. Even though there is already a plan for ocean drilling from two thousand twenty four to two thousand twenty nine, the Secretary must hold at least twenty new lease sales during the ten years after this law starts. The Secretary should try to follow a decision made in January two thousand seventeen about ocean drilling programs. The Secretary must hold these lease sales by specific dates twice a year from March two thousand twenty six through August two thousand thirty five. For each lease sale, the Secretary must offer at least seventy four million acres in the Gulf of Mexico area. This area is the same region shown in a government map from November two thousand sixteen. If someone sues about the environmental review of a lease sale, the lawsuit cannot cancel leases that were already sold or delay permits for drilling. If a court finds problems with the environmental review, the court cannot cancel the lease sale but must send it back to the Secretary to fix the problems. The Secretary must keep processing drilling permits during any lawsuit. Within sixty days of a lawsuit being filed, the Secretary must tell lease holders about the lawsuit. Lease holders can ask to pause their lease timelines while the lawsuit continues. This law also changes rules about drilling in the Eastern Gulf of Mexico. The ban on drilling in certain Gulf areas is extended until December thirty first two thousand thirty five. The ban now also covers the South Atlantic and Straits of Florida areas. However, the ban does not affect leases that already exist when this law passes. The Secretary can still give out leases in banned areas if they are for protecting the environment, such as protecting shores, beaches, wetlands, or wildlife habitats.
